summary: UR034 is a HYBRID ASTRONOMICAL-CADASTRAL REGISTER -- a MIXED DOCUMENT combining 3 astronomical markers (kaki Pleiades x2, maki Mars x1, maqa eclipse x4) with 3 cadastral markers (kaqa wall-AY x2, chiqa truth x2, taqa implicit). 188 cords, 1-PAIRE depth L4, 38 clusters, 5 colors (AB/W/MB/RL/LA). 16 STRING at 100 percent dict hit. FIRST WORD = chiqa (truth, C1 AB) = THESIS IS VERIFICATION, opening the document as a certification of observations and land-measurements. At C107 on GG (justice / governance colour) the scribe writes pitaka (who-ACC-PASS) = FORMAL INQUIRY 'who was assigned / responsible?'. DUAL-LEVEL TEXT distribution: 7 STRING on L1 (chiqa, pitaka, kaki, chi-DIR, maqa sv=10, maqa, TOP-pa sv=50) = PRIMARY labels; 9 STRING on L2-L4 (kaki, kaqa, chi-GEN, maki, kaqa, maqa x2, chiqa, chi-DIR) = ANNOTATIONS. Numbers primary, words explain. Likely an OBSERVATION-STATION SURVEY: a register of what the station observed (Pleiades, Mars, eclipses) AND where it was located (walls, boundaries). Consistent with Zuidema 1964 ceque system: huacas assigned to both astronomical duties and cadastral positions. Provenance unknown. Museo de la Nacion, Lima.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  188 cords. 16 STRING (9 percent, 100 percent dict hits), 159 INT, 13 EMPTY (7 percent).
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE, depth L4. 38 clusters.
  Colors: AB(77), W(52), MB(15), RL(15), LA(10). 5 colors.
  Provenance: UNKNOWN. Museum: Museo de la Nacion, Lima.

HYBRID VOCABULARY (3 astronomical + 3 cadastral)
  Astronomical:
    kaki x2 -- PLEIADES
    maki x1 -- MARS observation
    maqa x4 -- ECLIPSE (most frequent label)
  Cadastral:
    kaqa x2 -- WALL (Aymara)
    chiqa x2 -- TRUTH / verification
    (taqa boundary implicit)
  Interrogative:
    pitaka x1 -- WHO-ACC-PASS (formal inquiry, on GG colour) = 'who was assigned / responsible?'

FIRST WORD = chiqa (truth, C1 AB) -- VERIFICATION AS THESIS.
INTERROGATIVE = pitaka (C107 GG) -- formal responsibility question.
CLOSING = TOP-pa sv=50 (C188 W) -- topic marker with value 50 closes the document.

DUAL-LEVEL TEXT DISTRIBUTION
  L1 (7 STRING): chiqa (C1), pitaka (C107), kaki, chi-DIR, maqa sv=10, maqa, TOP-pa sv=50 (C188)
                 -- PRIMARY LABELS.
  L2-L4 (9 STRING): kaki, kaqa, chi-GEN, maki, kaqa, maqa x2, chiqa, chi-DIR
                 -- ANNOTATIONS. Numbers primary, words explain.
  The dual-level split matches the UR003 / UR278 observatory-reference convention: primary numerical
  data on L1, textual annotations on subsidiaries.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR034 is an OBSERVATION-STATION SURVEY: a register tracking both WHAT the station observed
  (Pleiades, Mars, eclipses) AND WHERE it was located (walls, boundaries, cadastral position). The
  hybrid vocabulary reflects the Inca ceque system (Zuidema 1964) where individual huacas were
  assigned both astronomical duties and cadastral positions -- a huaca was simultaneously a
  sacred-precinct landmark AND an observational post.
narrative:
Truth. That is the first word of UR034 -- chiqa, cord one, on light brown. The document opens as a certification. What follows is a register of numbers with scattered celestial and geographic labels, anchored by the verification thesis.

The Pleiades appear twice. Kaki. Once buried in the subsidiary tree at level two, once prominently on the main level. The scribe mentions the star cluster at both scales -- as an annotation and as a primary label. Mars, maki, appears once, in the tree. The eclipse marker, maqa, is the most frequent word in the document, four occurrences distributed across levels.

And alongside the sky, the ground. Kaqa, the Aymara word for wall, twice. Chiqa, truth / verification, twice more. Taqa, the boundary, is implicit in the structural segmentation. The register mixes what an observer sees overhead with what a surveyor measures below.

At cord one hundred and seven, on a gray cord of justice, the scribe writes pitaka -- who-accusative-passive. The formal interrogative, in passive-accusative: who was responsible? Who was assigned to this station? The Inca ceque system assigned individual huacas to specific duties -- an observational post might be staffed by an ayllu on rotation, its responsibility inscribed in the knot record.

The walls and the verification marks frame a document that is neither pure astronomy nor pure cadastre but something between. A register of what was observed and where. Who tracked the Pleiades rising. Who measured the wall's alignment. Who witnessed the eclipse. The huaca-observer at this specific station, named by pronoun on a gray cord.

The museum says 'provenance unknown.' The vocabulary says something more: somewhere in the Inca empire, an observer watched the Pleiades rise above an Aymara wall and recorded both on the same khipu. The document closes on cord one hundred and eighty-eight with TOP-pa, the topic marker carrying the value fifty. Whatever topic this fifty quantifies -- observations, days, cords of offering, metres of wall -- it was the closing total, the final stamp on a register that tracked both earth and sky.

prior_work.claim: The Inca ceque system of Cuzco assigned individual huacas to both astronomical observation and cadastral-ceremonial functions; a single huaca could be simultaneously a sacred-precinct landmark, an observational post, and a labor-rotation anchor.
prior_work.citation: Zuidema, R. T. (1964). The Ceque System of Cuzco; Zuidema, R. T. (1982). Catachillay: the role of the Pleiades and the Southern Cross in the Andes.

prior_work.claim: Sky/ground polysemy is a structural feature of Inca Quechua: the same root names a celestial object and a ground category linked by the astronomical signal (kaki = Pleiades + corn, qaqa = dark constellation + rocky terrain, maqa = eclipse + account-mark).
prior_work.citation: Urton, G. (1981). At the Crossroads of the Earth and the Sky; Sivan, J. (2026). sky/ground polysemy suppression rule.

prior_work.claim: Quechua interrogatives (pi, pitaka, piqa) appear in both juridical and administrative khipu contexts; pitaka (who-ACC-PASS) is specifically a formal responsibility-assignment form distinct from juridical pi-interrogatives.
prior_work.citation: Gonzalez Holguin, D. (1608). Vocabulario; Cerron-Palomino, R. (1987). Linguistica Quechua.

v3_contribution.claim: HYBRID DOCUMENT TYPE: 3 astronomical markers (kaki, maki, maqa) + 3 cadastral markers (kaqa, chiqa, taqa implicit) on the same document. Both signatures fire but NEITHER at full strength (3/10 astro, 3/8 cadastral). First corpus-documented instance of a balanced astronomical-cadastral hybrid.

CHIQA-THESIS OPENING: first word at C1 AB is chiqa (truth / verification). The document opens as a certification rather than as an observation or a survey. The register is framed as a CERTIFIED MIXED RECORD.

PITAKA INTERROGATIVE ON GG GOVERNANCE COLOUR at C107: the formal 'who-ACC-PASS' question appears on the justice colour, marking a FORMAL INQUIRY into responsibility. Distinguishes UR034 from a simple observation log by adding an administrative-inquiry layer.

DUAL-LEVEL TEXT DISTRIBUTION: 7 STRING on L1 (primary labels) + 9 STRING on L2-L4 (annotations). Matches the UR003 / UR278 observatory-reference convention -- numbers primary, words explanatory. Depth L4 is deeper than most astronomical khipus.

OBSERVATION-STATION SURVEY sub-type hypothesis: the hybrid vocabulary + pitaka-responsibility inquiry + L4 depth support reading UR034 as a register of both WHAT a ceque-system observational post observed AND WHERE the post was located. Consistent with Zuidema 1964 ceque integration.

TOP-PA CLOSING AT SV=50: the document's last cord carries the topicaliser TOP-pa with value 50 -- a closing total marker. Not a standard astronomical closing (which tends to be a celestial anchor) nor a standard cadastral closing (which tends to be a landmark word). Hybrid closing matches hybrid content.

evidence.test: TOP-pa closing convention on hybrid document

companions.note: Pachacamac astronomical catalog (90 cords, ~1481-1533). Closest astronomical cousin; UR034 adds a cadastral layer that UR1145 does not. Both share kaki + maki + maqa markers.
Arica cadastral register. UR034's cadastral layer (kaqa + chiqa) parallels UR058A's cadastral convention; UR034 is the hybrid that UR058A's pure-cadastral is not.
Incahuasi 162-year observatory (26x9 matrix). UR034's dual-level text (numbers L1, words L2-L4) matches UR278's observatory-reference convention. Both mix observational data with textual annotations.

Leymebamba agricultural calendar (769 cords). UR034's hybrid astronomical-cadastral signature parallels UR003's integrated sky-soil reading. UR003 is the rural-agricultural application; UR034 is the possible observation-station-cadastral application.
Pachacamac ceque / huaca registry (waqa x7). Structural parallel for the ceque-system integration that UR034 may be documenting at a different site.
Incahuasi UE 17 cadastral-astronomical hybrid. Sibling hybrid document. UR277 + UR034 are two instances of the rare astronomical-cadastral mixed class.

word_reading.note: 16 STRING at 100 percent dict hit. pitaka decomposition (pi + taka in ACC-PASS form) is grammatically principled. All core vocabulary (kaki, maki, maqa, kaqa, chiqa, TOP-pa) is corpus-stable.

document_type.note: astronomical_calendar classification is a best-fit compromise given the balanced hybrid vocabulary. Neither pure astronomy (3/10 astro markers, below the 5-marker threshold) nor pure cadastre (3/8 cadastral markers). The 'observation-station survey' sub-type hypothesis is plausible but not decisive.

narrative.note: The 'ceque-system observation post' reading is internally coherent and supported by Zuidema 1964. The specific huaca-observer identification is interpretive; pitaka on GG colour is the strongest individual narrative anchor (formal responsibility inquiry).

data_integrity.note: 100 percent dict hit on STRING cords. 7 percent empty cords (13/188) is low, consistent with a well-filled register. L4 depth is reliable. No Ascher layer summarised in the deep-read. Provenance unknown limits cross-reference options.

sections.title: Chiqa-Thesis Opening
sections.reading: C1 AB chiqa = FIRST WORD = TRUTH / VERIFICATION. The document opens as a certification. Early L1 + L2 cords establish the register's initial anchor points.

sections.title: Astronomical + Cadastral Labels Interleaved
sections.reading: Hybrid vocabulary distributed across 95 cords. kaki (Pleiades) on L1 and L2. kaqa (wall Aymara) in subsidiaries. maki (Mars) in tree. maqa sv=10 (eclipse) on L1. The sky and the wall are recorded in parallel.

sections.title: Pitaka Formal Inquiry
sections.reading: C107 GG pitaka = 'who-ACC-PASS?' on GOVERNANCE / JUSTICE colour. The document's formal responsibility inquiry -- who was assigned to this station? Unique GG cord in the document.

key_cords.note: FORMAL INQUIRY -- 'who was assigned/responsible?' on governance colour

sections.title: Closing Mixed Block + TOP-pa
sections.reading: Remaining 81 cords carry the tail vocabulary: additional maqa + chiqa + chi-DIR + chi-GEN forms across L1-L4. Last cord C188 TOP-pa sv=50 closes the register with a topicalised total value of 50.

key_cords.note: LAST CORD = TOPIC MARKER sv=50 (closing total)

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). sky/ground polysemy suppression rule + hybrid document class.
Zuidema, R. T. (1964). The Ceque System of Cuzco.
Zuidema, R. T. (1982). Catachillay: the role of the Pleiades and the Southern Cross.
Urton, G. (1981). At the Crossroads of the Earth and the Sky.
Gonzalez Holguin, D. (1608). Vocabulario -- pitaka interrogative form.
Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara -- kaqa (wall).
Museo de la Nacion, Lima -- UR034 catalogue record (provenance unknown).
Compare UR1145 (Pachacamac astronomy), UR058A (Arica cadastral), UR278 (Incahuasi observatory), UR003 (Leymebamba agricultural), UR203 (Pachacamac ceque), UR277 (Incahuasi hybrid sibling).
